coredata.dc:title: Specific quantification of atropine using molecularly imprinted polymer on graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Khataee, Alireza
Hassanzadeh, Javad
Kohan, Elmira
coredata.dc:description: Herein, development of a reliable and specific fluorometric assay was disclosed for the sensitive detection of atropine. The method was designed using the surface molecularly imprinted polymer on high fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Molecularly imprinted polymer capped GQDs (MIP-GQDs) were prepared through the common co-polymerization reaction of 3-(3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), act as the main functional and cross-linking monomers, respectively. The used template for this reaction was atropine. The created blue luminescent MIP-GQDs composite, which had a great affinity to adsorb atropine from the sample solution, could lead to a notable fluorescence quenching. In fact, GQDs act as the recognizing antenna for adsorbed atropine into the specific MIP sites. The linear association between the observed quenching effect and atropine concentration was exploited to design a selective assay to the detection of atropine. After optimization process, a linear calibration graph was achieved in the atropine concentration range of 0.5–300 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.22 ng mL−1. Exploitation of high specific MIP technique along with high fluorescent GQDs provided a highly selective and sensitive assay for atropine as a model analyte. It was adequately utilized for the analysis of atropine in biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Surface molecular imprinting
Tropane
Fluorescence
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the MIP Capped-graphene Quantum Dots
Analytical Usage of MIP-GQDs for the Detection of Atropine
Optimization Experimentations for the Determination of Atropine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM images for (a) GQDs and (b) MIP-GQDs; (c) SEM image for MIP-GQDs; (d) AFM image for GQDs; (e) XRD pattern for GQDs; (f) FTIR spectra for GQDs and MIP-GQDs; (g) UV–Vis absorption spectrum for GQDs; and (h) fluorescence emission spectra for GQDs using different excitation wavelength.

(a) Fluorescence spectra for GQDs, NIP-GQDs and MIP-GQDs and the effect of washing process [λex = 390 nm, washing process was done by 3% acetic acid]; (b) FT-IR spectra of synthesized MIP-GQDs before and after 3 step washing process.
Optimization of (a) pH [0.2 mg mL−1 MIP-GQDs, 15 min contact time] and (b) contact time [0.2 mg mL−1 MIP-GQDs, 2 mM Tris buffer, pH 7] for the detection of 100 ng mL−1 atropine.

Fluorescence emission spectra for MIP-GQDs after addition of atropine with different concentrations [a-m: 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 70, 100, 150, 200 and 300, respectively], and (b) corresponding calibration graph (condition: 0.2 mg mL−1 MIP-GQDs, 2 mM Tris buffer, pH 7, 10 min contact time and λex = 390 nm).

Effect of some other components (including drugs with similar structures with atropine or common drugs) on the response of probe developed for the determination of atropine (condition: 0.2 mg mL−1 MIP-GQDs, 2 mM Tris buffer, pH 7, 10 min contact time, 100 ng mL−1 atropine and λex = 390 nm).
Schematic design for the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer capped on the surface of graphene quantum dots (MIP-GQDs) and its application for the selective determination of atropine.
Summary for some described techniques for atropine determination and comparison with developed MIP-based method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ads-DNA/PDDA–TiO2NPs–MWCNTs/PGE: pencil graphite electrode modified with multiwall carbon nanotubes, titanium dioxide nanoparticles, and poly-dialyldimethylammonium chloride decorated with ds-DNA; GCE/Co3O4: glassy carbon electrodes modified with Co3O4 nanostructures; RTP: room-temperature phosphorescence: QDs: quantum dots; NS: nanospheres; ECL: electrochemiluminescence; CL: chemiluminescence; MIPHs: molecularly imprinted photonic hydrogels; Poly Dopamine-MIP/Mn-ZnS QDs: poly Dopamine-Coated Molecularly Imprinted Mn-Doped ZnS Quantum Dots; GQDs: graphene quantum dots; MIP: molecularly imprinted polymer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Limit of tolerance for interfering effect of some species existing in the determination process of 100 ng mL−1 atropine by developed method (in optimum condition).

row.entry: Al3+, Mn2+, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, Cl−
Acetate, glucose, ascorbic acid, uric acid, citrate
Cr3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+
Amino acids: glycine, alanine, arginine, glutamic acid, cysteine, leucine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the determination of atropine in plasma samples using developed method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Specific quantification of atropine using molecularly imprinted polymer on graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, development of a reliable and specific fluorometric assay was disclosed for the sensitive detection of atropine. The method was designed using the surface molecularly imprinted polymer on high fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Molecularly imprinted polymer capped GQDs (MIP-GQDs) were prepared through the common co-polymerization reaction of 3-(3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), act as the main functional and cross-linking monomers, respectively. The used template for this reaction was atropine. The created blue luminescent MIP-GQDs composite, which had a great affinity to adsorb atropine from the sample solution, could lead to a notable fluorescence quenching. In fact, GQDs act as the recognizing antenna for adsorbed atropine into the specific MIP sites. The linear association between the observed quenching effect and atropine concentration was exploited to design a selective assay to the detection of atropine. After optimization process, a linear calibration graph was achieved in the atropine concentration range of 0.5–300 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.22 ng mL−1. Exploitation of high specific MIP technique along with high fluorescent GQDs provided a highly selective and sensitive assay for atropine as a model analyte. It was adequately utilized for the analysis of atropine in biological samples.

Specific fluorometric assay for atropine based on the surface molecularly imprinted high fluorescent graphene quantum dots (MIP-GQDs).
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaloids are famous for their high noxious features and an associated background of poisoning, as a result of accidental or intentional food pollution [1]. Atropine (C17H23NO3, Fig. 1 ), is an important tropane alkaloid which naturally occurs in Solanaceae plants [2, 3]. Because of its anticholinergic, antispasmodic and antidote features, atropine has been vastly considered in clinical investigates [2, 4]. The pharmacological action of atropine is related to its obstructive effect on muscarine subtype acetylcholine receptors. It causes not only peripheral symptoms such as dilated pupils, tachycardia, and lessened gastrointestinal motility, but also disturbs the central nervous system [5]. The most usual medicinal form of atropine is sulfate type ((C17H23NO3)2·H2SO4·H2O), which is obtained from belladonna herb and used as a cholinergic drug in the treatment of organophosphate nerve agent and pesticide poisonings, arrhythmia, cardiopathy, Parkinsonism, uveitis, and amblyopia. It is also applied before systemic anesthesia and during surgery to decrease saliva production [2, 3, 6, 7]. Abnormal consumption of atropine is highly toxic and can cause atropism [8] or other side effect such as tachycardia, stupor, fever, delirium, atypical kidney function, thirstiness and difficulty in swallowing [3, 9]. The oral minimal toxic dose (TDLo) for human is as lower as 33 μg kg−1. It also can be fatal in few mg amounts [3]. Therefore, as an alkaloid threatening the human health and due to their distinct physiological and toxic effects, the reliable recognition and measurement of atropine are of great importance in medical and forensic toxicology. Chromatographic techniques, especially with mass detection systems, are the most reported methods for the determination of atropine [8, 10–12]. But, they contain laborious, costly and complicated procedures which make them unsuitable for rapid and on-site analytical purposes. Some capillary electrophoresis [13], electrochemical [2, 14–17] and spectroscopic techniques [3, 7, 9, 18, 19] have also been reported for the determination of atropine, but they suffer from low selectivity or sensitivity. So, the reliable detection of low levels of atropine in biological samples has made a challenge for the analysts.

Simple, low-cost and high sensitive fluorometric methods can be useful for this purpose. In this regards, high luminescent quantum dots (QDs) have attached widespread consideration for their distinctive features, including unique photo-stability, wide absorption and narrow emission spectra, great quantum efficiency and large Stokes shift [20, 21]. Semi-conductive QDs have appealed great consideration in recent decades for their unique electronic and optical properties. But, they can release highly toxic heavy metals, which confine their usages. Highly desirable and eco-friendly carbon-based QDs have been widely used, and provided an actual alternative to usual semi-conductive QDs [20]. For instance, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are superior to semi-conductive QDs, in terms of high photo-stability, outstanding fluorescence activity, very low toxicity and notable biocompatibility. Currently, they could constitute an attractive class of sensing devices [22–25]. Contrary to the semi-conductive QDs, optical properties of GQDs are potentially affected by any chemical modification for their solubility or functionalization intentions. This subject can be exploited for improving the fluorescence properties of GQDs. Furthermore, the availability of necessary ingredients for the synthesis of GQDs is higher and so GQDs are relatively cheaper than semi-conductive QDs.

The main limit in the application of GQD-based fluorescence sensors may be related to their low selectivity problem. In recent times, the grouping of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) QDs-based analytical tools was considered as an operative system to recognize important targets.

Surface imprinting technique is a promising process to design and fabrication of custom-built recipients with three-dimensional and specific cavities for the considered target molecules. The remarkable features of MIPs, such as easy and economic building process, suitable stability and high affinity, have made them superior to other functionalized materials [26, 27]. Recently, the MIP-based luminescent sensors have been greatly reported as the favorable and effective tools in analytical recognition [28, 29]. The combination of the specific MIP recipients and the exceptional features of GQDs can be attractive to design selective luminescent sensors [28]. Some application of GQDs as proper support for MIP layer has been reported [28, 30–34]. There are a few studies on the utilization of MIP technique for atropine detection [3, 35]. In a recent case, Abbasifar and Abdolraouf created a composite of MIP capped Mn-dopped ZnS QDs for atropine detection [3].

In the current study, development of a facile and reliable fluorometric assay based on the MIP capped GQDs (MIP-GQDs) was reported for atropine determination. GQDs were first produced by thermal treatment of the citric acid precursor. The MIP-GQDs were then formed through the co-polymerization of (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES, as the functional monomer) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, as the cross-linking monomer). Atropine was used as template molecules during the imprinting progression. After completing the reaction, non-covalently attached template molecules were effectually removed by a proper washing procedure. The abandoned three-dimensional cavities were matched with atropine template molecules in shape and functional groups. Therefore, they can selectively adsorbed and rebind the atropine molecules by hydrogen bonds via nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups. Besides, the emission intensity of MIP-GQDs was decreased by adsorbing atropine. The decreasing effect was proportional to the atropine concentration and considered as a platform for the facile detection of atropine. The specific feature of MIP technique and high emission of GQDs led to the highly selective and sensitive assay to measure atropine in the biological media. A summarized image in Scheme 1 indicates the overall design of assay.

Fluorescence experiments were accomplished using an LS-45 PerkinElmer spectrofluorometer (USA). The experiments for UV–visible absorption analyses was performed by a WPA Light-wave spectrophotometer (S2000, England). The size, morphology, and chemical features of the organized nanostructures were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany, HR-TEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FEG-SEM, Mira3, Czech Republic), X-ray diffraction spectrometry (XRD, D5000, Siemens, Germany, comprising a Cu Kα source), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany) and Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Ara AFM, Iran).

The chemicals were all attained in analytical grade and utilized without any extra purification. Glucose, ammonia solution (75%), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and ethanol were bought from Merck (Germany). Proper quantities of atropine sulfate pure powder (Alborz Daru pharmacy, Iran) was dissolved in deionized water to prepare the atropine stock solution (with a concentration of 100 μg mL−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the MIP Capped-graphene Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: First, the synthesis of GQDs was done according to the literature which included the thermal pyrolysis of glucose [36]. The glucose pure powder (6 g) was melted at about 180 °C, leading to the formation of a dark orange solution. The heating source was turned off and then, deionized water (25 mL) was added under continuous and vigorous stirring. Then, for the creation the MIP on the GQDs surface, 0.02 mmol of atropine sulfate as the template molecule as well as 500 μL GQDs solution and 0.08 mmol of APTES were added to 10 mL ethanol, and stirred for about 1 h. Afterward, TEOS (0.4 mmol) and catalyst solution (NH3, 100 μL) were respectively added, and then, the mixture was agitated for a 12 h time period. The produced MIP-GQDs was centrifuged (11,000 rpm, 15 min) and washed with deionized water to separate unreacted reagents. Also, the non-imprinted composite (NIP-GQDs) were prepared for the control experiments, via a same procedure but in the absence of atropine. For the elimination of template molecules from MIP-GQDs, they were washed several times with 3% acetic acid solution to get the same fluorescence intensity with NIP-GQDs one (see Section 3.2). The final MIP-GQDs composite was stable at least for 1 month at 4 °C.

The determination of atropine by fluorometric experimentations were done in the batch arrangement. Typically, a constant quantity (500 μL, 2 mg mL−1) of prepared MIP-GQDs was mixed with standard atropine (in diverse concentrations over the linear range of obtained calibration graph) or its sample solutions. The Tris buffer (500 μL, 0.02 M) was added to adjust the pH 7. Then, the mixtures were efficiently stirred and their volumes were precisely increased to 5 mL (using deionized water). The stirring was continued for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensities were recorded at 490 nm (λex = 390 nm).

Urine and plasma samples were obtained from volunteer healthy persons and stored at −20 °C until their usage. A definite amount of standard atropine solution was spiked to 100 μL samples and vortexed for 10 s. Deproteinization process was performed with acetonitrile (300 μL). The solutions were finally centrifuged (1500g, 5 min) and then diluted suitably by Tris buffer solution. An appropriate aliquot of prepared samples was employed for the analysis of atropine by detailed procedure in Section 2.3.

As presented in Scheme 1, the MIP-capped GQDs were simply prepared and used for the specific measurement of atropine in aqueous media. The synthesis process was rapid, simple, green and economic. High fluorescent GQDs were firstly obtained by thermal treatment of glucose precursor and then, they capped with a MIP layer. The selected template was atropine to create corresponding receiving sites in polymeric matrix. After an effective washing step to reject the template molecules, the MIP acted as specific recipients and was able to adsorb atropine molecules from its solution, with a high selectivity. In contrast, the GQDs located in the polymeric matrix acted as recognizing antenna for the adsorbed atropine, which can quench the fluorescence emission of QDs. To the best of our knowledge, no MIP-based detection method has been reported for atropine by carbon nanoparticles.

The morphology and dimension of GQDs and its composite with MIP were assessed by TEM and AFM methods. The obtained TEM images revealed the almost monodisperse particles with average diameters of 18.5 ± 3.2 nm and 180 ± 15 nm for GQDs and MIP- GQDs, respectively (Fig. 2a, b). This means that more than one GQDs might be trapped into the MIP polymeric layer. Also, according to the HR-TEM image for one particle (inset of Fig. 2a), GQDs had a clear crystalline structure with a lattice constant of 0.24 nm, correlated to the graphene (1120) lattice fringe [36, 37]. The SEM image for the prepared MIP-GQDs (Fig. 2c) reveals the almost spherical particles with a constant diameter of approximately 190 nm and rough surfaces. This is in a good agreement with TEM outcomes (Fig. 2b). Also, based on the AFM results (Fig. 2d), the GQDs included a sheet structure with a thickness of about 0.9–2.5 nm which conforming the mono- or few-layered structures. In the XRD pattern of GQDs (Fig. 2e), a wide diffraction peak was located at 18° (002), demonstrating the graphite-like building of prepared QDs [38].

The study of the chemical structure using the FT-IR method (Fig. 2f) displayed some certain peaks for GQDs located at wavenumbers of about 1644 (stretching form of CO bonds), 1364 (stretching form of CC bonds) and 2930 (stretching form of CH bonds). Also, the OH and CO bonds formed a strong and broad peak at 3311 cm−1 and a peak at 1022 cm−1, respectively. After the creation of MIP polymeric layer, two specific and relatively intense bands for SiO bonds of polymer were observed at about 1110 and 1022 cm−1. A definite band also appeared at 1620 cm−1 corresponding to the NH bonds of APTES monomers.

The optical studies obtained a specific UV–vis absorption spectrum for GQDs including two well-known peaks at about 225 and 277 nm (Fig. 2g). The observed peaks for GQDs are as a result of the π → π⁎ and n → π⁎ transitions in the aromatic moieties and surface carbonyl functionalities, respectively [36, 39]. Moreover, intense fluorescence emanation was obtained for GQDs (Fig. 2h). The maximum wavelength of emission spectra (λem) was dependent on the employed excitation wavelength (λex), and a red shift was experienced in the fluorescence spectrum of GQDs by increasing λex. The maximum fluorescence intensity was detected in λem = 490 nm using a λex of 390 nm (Fig. 2h). So, 390 nm was chosen as the best excitation wavelength for fluorescence experimentations. It can lead to a high quantum efficiency. To assess the quantum efficiency, rhodamine B was applied as a proper reference material which obtained the amounts of 45% and 36% for GQDs and MIP-GQDs, respectively.

Finally, the stability of emission intensity for MIP-GQDs was studied during the time. The results displayed a constant fluorescence intensity for at least 25 days. The fluorescence data were collected for ten repeated determinations for MIP-GQDs in a constant concentration that obtained acceptable repeatability with the relative standard deviations (RSD) in the range of 1.8%–2.6%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical Usage of MIP-GQDs for the Detection of Atropine
ce:section.ce:para: A specific and reliable recognition system for atropine was designed using the synthesized MIP-GQDs. For this purpose, the surface imprinting process was implemented using the atropine molecules as templates. The process comprised the self-assembly co-polymerization of TEOS and APTES precursors which acted as the cross-linking and main monomers, respectively. The GQDs as suitable supports were also added during the polymerization step, resulted in their entrapping into the polymeric matrix and then producing the luminescent MIP-GQDs particles. After that, an effectual washing stage was done to dismiss the non-covalently attached template molecules. The linkage of atropine template molecules in MIP sites was probably due to the hydrogen bonds between the nitrogen or oxygen moieties of atropine with amino groups of APTES monomers. Application of a mild acidic condition (3% acetic acid solution) can eliminate this interaction and disengage the template molecules. The washing process to withdraw the atropine molecules from the polymeric MIP sites has been controlled by fluorescence emission intensity of MIP-GQDs. The washing step was repeated for 3 times to obtain a fluorescence intensity same with the NIP-GQDs one (synthesized similar to MIP-GQDs, but without atropine, see Section 2.2) (Fig. 3a). Fig. 3a shows that the fluorescence intensity of MIP-GQDs nearly becomes equal to the NIP-GQDs, after 3 washing stages. It means that the analyte molecules are almost completely released. Afterward, the remained acetic acid was removed by dispersion of MIP-GQDs particles in deionized water. The elimination of atropine molecules was also investigated by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3b). As can be clearly seen from Fig. 3b, the specific absorption peaks of atropine (at the wavenumbers of about 694, 1032 and 1714 cm−1) disappeared after washing steps.

Now, the MIP-GQDs composite contains specific unoccupied sites with configuration and functional groups corresponding to the atropine molecules. Consequently, MIP layer had a great affinity to atropine and guaranteed its selective adsorbing from solution without the serious interfering effects from similar compounds. In this assembly, GQDs acted as an optical antenna to distinguish the engaged atropine molecules into the MIP situates. The fluorescence emission of MIP-GQDs was quenched by atropine, and lower fluorescence intensities were obtained in the presence of higher concentrations of atropine. So, the association can be applied as a design for the facile, rapid and cost-effective detection of atropine. Improved sensitivity can be warranted by applied high luminescent GQDs.

The quenching effect of atropine on the fluorescence emission of GQDs can be related to its electron donor property, which can disturb the electronic features of GQDs. Therefore, it can be considered that the electron-hole pairs of GQDs can be affected by nearby atropine molecules in MIP sites, leading to the changing of fluorescence emission intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization Experimentations for the Determination of Atropine
ce:section.ce:para: The main factors, affecting the decreasing signal of developed system for atropine (ΔF = F0 − F; F0 and F show the fluorescence emission intensity of MIP-GQDs in the absence and presence of atropine, respectively), were investigated to obtain an optimized condition and improve the sensitivity. For this aim, the effects of pH and contact time between atropine solution and MIP-GQDs were considered (Fig. 4 ).

To investigate the effect of pH, the experiments were done in different pH values ranged from 4 to 12, and the fluorescence signals (ΔF) were followed for a fixed concentration of atropine (100 ng mL−1) and same contact time of 15 min (Fig. 4a). The maximum ΔF was obtained in the pH values of 6–9. The change in the functional groups of atropine or MIP sites and then poorer interactions between them is may be the reason for the lower signals in acidic or basic pHs. The most appropriate buffer to adjust pH of the solution (pH 7) was Tris (tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane). Other applied buffers (potassium hydrogen phosphate, sodium bicarbonate, and ammonium acetate) generated very lower signals.

The effect of contact time for the adsorption of atropine molecules in MIP sites was also studied. The MIP-GQDs dispersion was exposed to the atropine solution (100 ng mL−1 atropine and pH 7) for various time periods, and then, its fluorescence intensity was followed (Fig. 4b). A rapid lessening in the emission intensity was observed in first 8 min, and then it reached an almost constant value. So, we applied 10 min for subsequent experiments as the optimum contact time.

It is worth to mention that the selected concentration for MIP-GQDs was 0.2 mg mL−1, as it obtained the highest emission intensity (but not-overloaded), which led to a higher dynamic range for the determination of atropine.

The experiments evidenced a remarkable decrease in the fluorescent intensity of MIP-GQDs by adding atropine even in trace concentrations. The observed diminishing effect was proportional to the atropine concentration, and lower fluorescence intensities were achieved for the higher concentrations of atropine (Fig. 5a). The linear calibration graph was plotted as the ΔF values against the atropine concentration (Fig. 5b) in the concentration range of 0.5–230 ng mL−1 atropine (ΔF = 2.128C + 18.86; R2 = 0.9987), with a detection limit (3Sb/m, Sb is the standard deviation for the determination of F0 (n = 5), and m is the calibration slope) of 0.22 ng mL−1. The precise and reproducibility of the method were assessed by repeated determinations of 10 and 100 ng mL−1 atropine that obtained acceptable RSD values (2.84 and 1.37%, respectively).

In order to compare the analytical features for developed method and some other previously reported procedures, they were listed in Table 1 . It is clear that the presented method has a relatively good sensitivity as well as simple synthesis or determination process. Moreover, great specificity was obtained by MIP technique. So, it can provide a rapid, reliable and on-line recognition system for screening purposes, without any interfering effect. Also, the method require no additional pretreatment steps. Moreover, compared with a similar work containing the application of MIP capped Mn-dopped ZnS QDs for the determination of atropine [3], the presented work exploit GQDs, which have lower toxicity. By the way, the presented system is a novel sensing probe and can be improved and extended for the successful determination of other significant analytes.

The likely interfering behavior of some species, which can exist in the real samples, was examined in the analytical process for atropine determination. The experiments were performed via the analysis of atropine solution (100 ng mL−1) according to the mentioned general procedure (Section 2.3) after addition of increasing concentrations of considered species. The tolerable interfering ratios (interference concentration/atropine concentration in ng mL−1) was considered for relative errors of <5% (Table 2 ). The actual concentration of each material in the considered real samples was below the mentioned ones in Table 2. On the other hand, according to the results, no significant interfering effect was observed for the tested material in stated concentrations. So, an interference-free measurement of atropine in these real samples is expected.

By the way, the response of MIP-GQDs was examined for some other drugs, especially for compounds with a similar structure to atropine (Fig. 6 ). According to the obtained results, no considerable changes in the fluorescence intensity were observed for even 50 μg mL−1 of tested drugs. So, the high selectivity of MIP-GQDs toward atropine was verified. However, the fluorescence intensity of NIP-GQDs was affected by almost all of compounds (but create a lower ΔF in comparing with MIP-GQDs). This subject demonstrated the high importance of MIP layer in the developed assay.

The reliability of the designed assay for atropine detection was evaluated by analysis of some human plasma samples. The recovery values are shown in Table 3 , which are in the satisfactory range (98.2–102.9%). So, the developed method has favorable accuracy for the quantification of atropine in real samples.

High fluorescent GQDs was applied as suitable support to prepare polymeric MIP layer. The MIP-GQDs were successfully produced through the self-assembly reaction of APTES and TEOS as polymerization precursors, and atropine was used as the template. The characterizing analysis verified the success of the synthesis experiments, too. The entrapping GQDs into the polymeric layer caused the formation of MIP particles with a great fluorescence emission. Besides, application of atropine as the template of imprinting process led to the creation of specific recipient sites for atropine. So, a selective adsorbing affinity toward atropine was created in MIP-GQDs particles. On the other hand, locating the atropine molecules in MIP sites caused a remarkable quenching effect in the emission intensity of nearby GQDs, corresponding with the atropine concentration. This effect was applied for the selective quantification of atropine in human plasma samples. The developed method was able to detect the atropine concentration in the range of 0.5–230 ng mL−1, without any interfering effect from other drugs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the University of Tabriz for all of the supports provided.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alkaloids in the human food chain–natural occurrence and possible adverse effects
Mol. Nutr. Food Res.
Determination of atropine sulfate using a novel sensitive DNA–biosensor based on its interaction on a modified pencil graphite electrode
Talanta
Selective determination of atropine using poly dopamine-coated molecularly imprinted Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots
J. Fluoresc.
Molecularly imprinted photonic hydrogels for fast screening of atropine in biological samples with high sensitivity
Forensic Sci. Int.
Modern Alkaloids: Structure, Isolation, Synthesis, and Biology
Direct and sensitive determination of atropine sulfate at polymer electrode in presence of surface active agents
Int. J. Electrochem. Sci.
Flow-injection post chemiluminescence determination of atropine sulfate
Anal. Chim. Acta
Measurement of atropine and scopolamine in hair by LC–MS/MS after Datura stramonium chronic exposure
Forensic Sci. Int.
Determination of atropine using Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots as novel luminescent sensitizers
J. Lumin.
Analysis of toxic alkaloids in body samples
Forensic Sci. Int.

Food Chem.
